en: Toad. Taste pungent, cold nature. Indicated for pathogenic qi; breaks up hard masses and stagnant blood, treats abscesses, swellings, and genital sores. Taken long-term, prevents febrile disease. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Hair and hoof-nails of the six domestic animals. Taste salty, neutral nature. Indicated for ghost-attachment and gu poisoning, chills and fever, fright epilepsy, and madness with convulsions and manic wandering. Camel hair is especially good.
Flying squirrel. Induces abortion and eases childbirth.

Elk fat. Taste pungent, warm nature. Indicated for abscesses, swellings, malignant sores, and necrotic flesh; cold-wind damp painful obstruction, stiffness and contracture of the four limbs, wind-swelling of the head with qi stagnation; it opens the skin's interstices. Also called gong fat. Grows in mountain valleys.

Pig testicles. Taste sweet, warm nature. Indicated for fright epilepsy and madness, ghost-attachment and gu poisoning; relieves chills and fever, running-piglet qi surges, the five stranguries, pathogenic qi, and spasmodic contraction. The dew-claw treats the five types of hemorrhoids, hidden heat lodged in the intestines, and intestinal abscess with internal erosion. Also called tún diān.

Swallow droppings. Taste pungent, neutral nature. Indicated for gu poisoning and ghost-attachment; expels inauspicious pathogenic qi, breaks the five stranguries, and disinhibits urination. Found in high valleys and level valleys.

Bat droppings. Taste pungent, cold nature. Indicated for swelling and abscess of the face and eyes, recurring skin pain, blood-qi disorders in the abdomen; breaks up accumulations from cold and heat, and relieves fright palpitations. Also called shǔgū, also called shígān (stone liver). Grows in the mountain valleys of Hepu.

Hornet's nest. Taste bitter, neutral nature. Indicated for fright epilepsy with convulsions, chills and fever from pathogenic qi, madness, ghost essence and gu poisoning, and intestinal hemorrhoids. Best prepared roasted over fire. Also called fēngchǎng. Grows in mountain valleys.

Chū jī (ailanthus lanternfly). Taste bitter, neutral nature. Indicated for pathogenic qi in the heart and abdomen, and impotence; boosts essence, strengthens willpower, aids conception, and enhances sexual vigor. Tonifies the middle and lightens the body. Grows in river valleys.

Zhè chóng (wingless ground beetle). Taste salty, cold nature. Indicated for recurring chills and fever in the heart and abdomen, blood accumulations and abdominal masses; breaks up hardness and expels blocked menstrual blood. Excellent for aiding conception. Also called dìbiē. Grows in river marshes.
Leech. Taste salty, neutral nature. Expels malign and stagnant blood and blocked menstruation; breaks up blood masses and accumulations, treats infertility, and disinhibits the waterways.

Shícán (caddisfly larva). Taste sour, non-toxic. Indicated for the five stranguries; breaks the five types of painful urinary dribbling, and induces abortion. Its flesh resolves bound qi, disinhibits the waterways, and clears heat. Also called shāshī. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Snake slough. Taste salty, neutral nature. Indicated for the 120 kinds of infantile fright epilepsy, convulsions, madness, chills and fever, intestinal hemorrhoids, worm toxicity, and snake-epilepsy. Best prepared roasted over fire. Also called lóngzǐyī, shéfú, lóngzǐdānyī, and gōngpí (bow-skin). Grows in mountain valleys.

Centipede. Taste pungent, warm nature. Indicated for ghost-attachment and gu poisoning; counteracts venom from snakes, insects, and fish, kills malevolent spirits and old specters, treats warm malaria, and expels the three worms. Grows in river valleys.

Millipede. Taste pungent, warm nature. Indicated for severe hard steaming heat in the abdomen; breaks up accumulations and masses, treats polyps, malignant sores, and scalp ringworm. Also called bǎizú (hundred-legs). Grows in river valleys.

Yìwēng (mud-dauber wasp). Taste pungent, neutral nature. Indicated for chronic deafness, cough with counterflow qi, and toxic qi; draws out embedded splinters, and induces sweating. Grows in river valleys.
Quèwèng (moth cocoon). Taste sweet, neutral nature. Indicated for infantile fright epilepsy, chills and fever with bound qi, gu poisoning, and ghost-attachment. Also called zàoshè.
Bǐzǐ. Taste sweet, warm nature. Indicated for pathogenic qi in the abdomen; expels the three worms, treats snake bites, gu poisoning, ghost-attachment, and hidden corpse (a wasting disease).

Shǔfù (pillbug/woodlouse). Taste sour, warm nature. Indicated for qi strangury with inability to urinate, blocked menstruation and blood masses in women, epilepsy and convulsions with chills and fever; disinhibits the waterways. Also called pánzhēn, also called yīxián. Grows in level valleys.

Firefly. Taste pungent, slightly warm nature. Brightens the eyes; treats fire-sore injuries in children and heat qi, gu poisoning, and ghost-attachment; opens the spirit-essence. Also called yèguāng (night light). Grows in ponds and marshes.

Silverfish. Taste salty, warm nature, non-toxic. Indicated for hernia-like abdominal masses in women, difficult urination, and infantile wind-strike with stiff neck — for all of these it should be applied as a rub. Also called báiyú (white fish). Grows in level marshes.

White-necked earthworm. Taste salty, cold nature. Indicated for snake-type abdominal masses; expels the three worms and hidden corpse, ghost-attachment and gu poisoning; kills long worms (roundworms). It can also dissolve itself into water. Grows in level ground.

Mole cricket. Taste salty, cold nature. Indicated for difficult childbirth; draws out splinters embedded in the flesh, ruptures abscesses and swellings, relieves choking obstruction in the throat, resolves toxicity, and treats malignant sores. Those caught at night are best. Also called huìgū, tiānlóu, and hú. Grows in level marshes.

Dung beetle. Taste salty, cold nature. Indicated for infantile fright epilepsy with convulsions, abdominal distension with chills and fever, and adult madness and erratic behavior. Best prepared roasted over fire. Also called jiéqiāng. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Blister beetle. Taste pungent, cold nature. Indicated for chills and fever from ghost-attachment and gu poisoning, scrofula, malignant sores, erosive ulcers, and necrotic flesh; breaks up stony strangury. Also called lóngwěi (dragon tail). Grows in river valleys.

Dìdǎn (oil beetle). Taste pungent, cold nature. Indicated for chills and fever from ghost-attachment, scrofula and malignant sores, and necrotic accumulations; breaks up abdominal masses, and induces abortion. Also called yuánqīng. Grows in river valleys.
